title: Anomaly Detection Revisited
concept: Callback to Unit 172: autoencoders trained only on normal data reconstruct anomalies poorly, making reconstruction error a natural anomaly signal.

exercises.prompt: Quick check: what does an autoencoder try to do?

options.text: Copy its input back out as closely as possible

exercises.explain: Right! An autoencoder squeezes data down and rebuilds it, trying to match the original as closely as possible.

cards.title: Something Looks... Off
cards.body: Imagine a security guard who has only ever seen normal, everyday visitors. The moment someone weird and out-of-place walks in, the guard instantly notices something's wrong — even without being told what 'wrong' looks like. That's exactly how autoencoders catch anomalies!

cards.title: Reconstruction Error as a Signal
cards.body: If an autoencoder is trained ONLY on normal data, it becomes really good at rebuilding normal things — but bad at rebuilding weird, unusual ones. The 'error' (difference between input and rebuilt output) is small for normal data and large for anomalies. So a big reconstruction error = a red flag that something unusual just showed up.

cards.title: The Portrait Artist
cards.body: Picture an artist who has spent years only painting portraits of dogs. If you show them a cat and ask them to redraw it from memory, they'll mess it up — maybe paint a dog with strange fur! That messy redrawing is like reconstruction error: the artist (autoencoder) struggles because the cat (anomaly) is nothing like what they practiced on.

cards.title: Factory Machine Sensors
cards.body: A factory trains an autoencoder on sensor readings from machines running normally for months. One day, a machine starts overheating in a strange new pattern. The autoencoder tries to reconstruct this reading but fails badly — the reconstruction error spikes, alerting engineers to check the machine before it breaks down.

cards.title: Myth: You Need Labeled Anomalies to Detect Them
cards.body: Myth: You must show the model lots of examples of 'bad' or anomalous data to catch anomalies. Truth: Autoencoders only need NORMAL data during training! They never see anomalies beforehand — they just get worse at reconstructing anything that looks different from normal, which itself reveals the anomaly.

cards.title: Normal vs. Anomaly Reconstruction
cards.body: The same autoencoder handles two different inputs very differently, based only on how similar they are to what it was trained on.

left.text: Input closely matches training patterns → rebuilt almost perfectly → tiny reconstruction error

right.text: Input looks unfamiliar → autoencoder struggles to rebuild it → large reconstruction error

exercises.prompt: Why does an autoencoder trained only on normal data struggle with anomalies?

options.text: It was never trained on anything resembling anomalies, so it can't rebuild them well
Anomalies are always bigger files than normal data

exercises.explain: Correct! The autoencoder only learned patterns from normal data, so unfamiliar (anomalous) inputs are hard for it to reconstruct accurately.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A large reconstruction error usually signals that the input might be an anomaly.
exercises.explain: True! Since the model reconstructs normal data well, a big mismatch (error) between input and output hints that the data is unusual.

exercises.sentence: Autoencoders trained only on normal data produce a large reconstruction ____ when they encounter anomalies.
exercises.explain: The gap between the input and the reconstructed output is called the reconstruction error — and it grows large for unfamiliar, anomalous inputs.

exercises.prompt: Which training approach lets an autoencoder detect anomalies without ever seeing labeled anomaly examples?

options.text: Train it only on normal data, then flag high reconstruction error as anomalies
Train it on a 50/50 mix of normal and anomaly data with labels
Train it only on anomaly examples so it recognizes them directly

exercises.explain: Exactly! Training only on normal data means the model becomes an expert at normal patterns, so anything it reconstructs poorly stands out as likely anomalous — no labeled anomalies needed.

exercises.prompt: A hospital wants to detect unusual heart rhythm patterns using an autoencoder, but doctors only have plenty of examples of NORMAL heart rhythms — very few abnormal ones. What's the best strategy?

options.text: Train the autoencoder only on normal rhythms, then flag rhythms with high reconstruction error as possibly abnormal
Wait until enough abnormal examples are collected before training anything
Train the autoencoder on abnormal rhythms only, since those are what matters
Skip the autoencoder and randomly guess which rhythms are abnormal

exercises.explain: Correct! This is the whole point of the technique: since normal data is abundant, train the autoencoder to master reconstructing it. Then any rhythm that gets poorly reconstructed (high error) is flagged as a potential anomaly — no need to wait for rare abnormal examples.
